summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: Carbon-14 dating is used to determine the age of:

Carbon-14 dating measures the ratio of C-14 to C-12 in organic materials. C-14 has a half-life of ~5,730 years, making it useful for dating materials up to ~50,000 years old. The answer is 'Organic materials up to ~50,000 years old'. The other options — 'Metals', 'Living organisms only', and 'Rocks billions of years old' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

Example: The CANDU reactor uses heavy water (D₂O) instead of regular water. The advantage is:

Heavy water moderates neutrons effectively but absorbs far fewer neutrons than H₂O. This allows CANDU reactors to run on unenriched natural uranium (0.7% U-235).

Example: The main advantage of nuclear power over fossil fuels is:

1 kg of U-235 produces as much energy as ~1500 tonnes of coal (via E=mc²). No CO₂ is emitted during operation, though mining, construction, and waste management have environmental impacts.

Example: The primary goal of the ITER fusion project is to:

ITER's goal: achieve plasma Q ≥ 10 (500 MW output from 50 MW input) — proving fusion can produce net energy. ITER is expected to operate in the 2030s.
